heroOverview: Bromhidrosis is the production of offensively malodorous sweat caused by bacterial decomposition of eccrine or apocrine secretions on the skin surface, affecting roughly 0.5–1% of the general population to a clinically significant degree. The condition is most commonly apocrine in origin — centred on the axillae, groin, and perianal area — where bacteria convert odourless apocrine lipid precursors into short-chain fatty acids and thioalcohols, the compounds responsible for characteristic body odour. Eccrine bromhidrosis, less common, involves volatile compounds excreted through the much more numerous eccrine sweat glands and is often systemic in origin, linked to metabolic disorders such as trimethylaminuria, rare amino acid defects, or ingestion of odorous foods. Although not physically harmful, bromhidrosis consistently ranks among the most psychologically distressing dermatological conditions, with documented high rates of anxiety, social withdrawal, and impaired occupational function.

keyStats.prevalence: Clinically significant bromhidrosis is estimated to affect 0.5–1% of the population; mild body odour detectable by others is far more prevalent but not classified as a disorder
keyStats.demographics: Apocrine bromhidrosis is most common in young adults aged 15–35 when apocrine glands are most active; eccrine forms affect all age groups
keyStats.avgAge: Onset most frequently in adolescence or early adulthood when apocrine secretion begins; some forms persist lifelong without treatment
keyStats.globalCases: Trimethylaminuria (fish odour syndrome), a systemic cause of eccrine bromhidrosis, is estimated to affect approximately 1 in 200,000 people globally

definition: Bromhidrosis (ICD-10: L75.0) is a disorder of apocrine or eccrine sweat glands characterised by the production of malodorous secretions. The term derives from the Greek bromos (stench) and hidros (sweat). It is classified by gland of origin into two main subtypes: apocrine bromhidrosis, by far the most common, arises from the action of skin-surface bacteria — primarily Corynebacterium species and coagulase-negative Staphylococci — on the odourless fatty acid and steroid precursors secreted by apocrine glands in the axillae, groin, perianal area, and areolae, converting them to the short-chain fatty acids (principally 3-methyl-2-hexenoic acid), androstenone, androstenol, and thioalcohols that constitute human body odour; eccrine bromhidrosis, less common, occurs when volatile malodorous compounds are excreted systemically through the eccrine glands, most notably in trimethylaminuria (fish-odour syndrome), phenylketonuria, and hepatic or renal failure, or when ingested substances such as garlic, fenugreek, or curry compounds are secreted in sweat. Bromhidrosis falls under apocrine sweat disorders in the ICD classification and is primarily managed by dermatology, though eccrine forms may require metabolic or gastroenterological involvement. It must be distinguished from osmhidrosis (a historical synonym) and from olfactory reference syndrome, a psychiatric condition in which the patient is convinced they emit an odour that others cannot detect. The condition is neither infectious nor contagious.

typesClassification.description: The dominant form, affecting the axillae, groin, perianal area, and areolae. Corynebacterium and staphylococcal bacteria convert apocrine secretion into volatile fatty acids and thioalcohols. Aggravated by heavy sweating, poor hygiene, tight clothing, and hair in the axillae.

Volatile malodorous compounds excreted through eccrine glands either from a systemic metabolic disorder (trimethylaminuria, phenylketonuria, tyrosinaemia) or from ingestion of odorant-containing foods (garlic, onion, fenugreek, alcohol, some spices). The odour may be generalised rather than limited to apocrine gland distribution areas.
Features of both apocrine and eccrine subtypes, common in patients with both hyperhidrosis (increased eccrine output) and dense apocrine gland areas with active bacterial colonisation.

primarySymptoms: Persistent, offensive body odour from the axillae, groin, perianal area, or generalised skin surface, detectable to others at conversational distance.
Malodour that returns rapidly — within hours — after bathing, distinguishing clinically significant bromhidrosis from simple hygiene-related odour.
Odour that worsens noticeably with physical activity, warm weather, emotional stress, or sexual arousal, which trigger apocrine secretion.
A characteristic sharp, pungent, or rancid quality of the axillary odour, produced by bacterial breakdown of fatty acid precursors into 3-methyl-2-hexenoic acid.
A fishy odour affecting the whole body including the breath, urine, and sweat in patients with trimethylaminuria (fish-odour syndrome).
Staining or discolouration of clothing in the axillary region from chromhidrosis-associated apocrine pigment, which co-occurs in some patients.
Social anxiety, avoidance of public spaces and physical contact, and fear of detection by others — typically the most impairing aspect of the condition.
Skin maceration, irritation, or intertrigo in the affected folds, secondary to the moist environment that promotes bacterial overgrowth.
Psychological symptoms including depressed mood, reduced self-confidence, and, in severe cases, occupational impairment or social isolation.

In eccrine bromhidrosis from systemic causes, associated systemic features of the underlying condition — jaundice in hepatic failure, uraemic symptoms in renal failure, or developmental delay in untreated metabolic disorders.
earlyWarningSigns: Body odour returning within 2–4 hours of a thorough shower, suggesting apocrine secretion has resumed and bacteria are actively metabolising it.
Family members or close contacts consistently commenting on an odour despite regular washing.
Clothing in the axillary region becoming permanently stained or malodorous even after laundering.
New-onset fishy body odour after a high-protein diet or around menstruation, which can flag trimethylaminuria as a cause.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of a strong, sweet, fruity, or acetone-like body odour in a person with diabetes — may indicate diabetic ketoacidosis, a medical emergency.
New-onset fishy odour combined with jaundice, confusion, and abdominal distension — hepatic failure requires urgent hospital assessment.
Severe uraemic odour (ammonia-like) with oliguria, oedema, and extreme fatigue — acute kidney injury or end-stage renal disease.
Signs of secondary cellulitis in bromhidrotic skin folds — spreading erythema, warmth, pain, and fever — requiring prompt antibiotic treatment.

causes.cause: Bacterial metabolism of apocrine secretions (primary cause)
causes.description: Apocrine glands secrete a rich, odourless oily fluid containing proteins, steroids, and long-chain fatty acids. Skin-resident Corynebacterium species and coagulase-negative staphylococci produce enzymes that cleave these precursors into volatile short-chain fatty acids — principally 3-methyl-2-hexenoic acid — androstenone, androstenol, and thioalcohols, which are the principal malodorous compounds. Axillary hair increases the surface area available for bacterial colonisation and odour production.

Elevated eccrine sweat output from primary hyperhidrosis creates a persistently moist skin microenvironment that greatly accelerates bacterial growth and metabolite production. The combination of hyperhidrosis and dense apocrine gland areas is a powerful driver of severe bromhidrosis; treating hyperhidrosis with antiperspirants or botulinum toxin typically substantially reduces the associated odour.

A defect in the flavin-containing monooxygenase 3 (FMO3) enzyme prevents hepatic oxidation of dietary trimethylamine (derived from choline, lecithin, carnitine, and marine foods) to its odourless N-oxide form. Unmetabolised trimethylamine accumulates and is excreted through eccrine sweat, urine, and expired air as a strong fish-like odour. Mutations in the FMO3 gene produce the primary form; acquired temporary forms occur with high-choline diets, during menstruation, and in liver disease.

Uncontrolled diabetes produces elevated levels of ketone bodies — acetone, acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate — that are excreted through eccrine sweat and breath, producing a characteristic sweet, fruity, or nail-polish-remover odour. This form of eccrine bromhidrosis can resolve with optimised glycaemic control.

Garlic (allicin and its metabolites), onion, curry spices (fenugreek, asafoetida), alcohol, and some cruciferous vegetables produce volatile compounds that are absorbed from the gut, circulated to skin, and excreted through eccrine sweat. This transient eccrine bromhidrosis resolves 24–48 hours after the food is cleared.

causes.cause: Poor skin hygiene and infrequent bathing
causes.description: When apocrine secretions are not removed promptly, bacterial colonisation proceeds unchecked and metabolite concentrations accumulate progressively on the skin surface. Daily bathing, particularly of apocrine-dense areas, is a modifiable component of virtually every case.

Chronic kidney disease produces uraemic frost — urea crystallising on the skin surface — and excretes volatile amine compounds through eccrine sweat, producing the characteristic ammonia-like odour of uraemia. Hepatic failure excretes dimethyl sulphide and other sulfur compounds through eccrine sweat and breath (fetor hepaticus).

riskFactors.description: Excessive eccrine sweating creates the moist warm environment that accelerates bacterial metabolism of apocrine secretions and amplifies odour production. Primary focal hyperhidrosis is present in a substantial proportion of patients with severe bromhidrosis; treating it with topical aluminium salts or botulinum toxin significantly reduces odour intensity.

Higher body weight increases skin fold depth in the axillae, groin, and inframammary areas, trapping moisture and heat that accelerate bacterial growth. Skin folds create anaerobic microenvironments where Corynebacterium and anaerobic cocci thrive, producing sulphur-containing compounds additional to the standard fatty acid metabolites.

Axillary hair increases bacterial surface area threefold to fivefold, amplifying apocrine odour production. Shaving the axillae reduces odour measurably by limiting colonisable surface and improving antiperspirant contact with skin.

Carriers of the ABCC11 wet-earwax allele (dominant in populations of African and European ancestry, nearly absent in East Asian populations) have markedly more active axillary apocrine secretion. The dry-earwax variant is associated with virtual absence of apocrine bromhidrosis, explaining the low prevalence of axillary odour in East Asian populations.

riskFactors.factor: Trimethylaminuria or other inborn metabolic errors
riskFactors.description: FMO3 gene variants that reduce flavin-containing monooxygenase 3 activity cause trimethylamine accumulation and fish-odour syndrome. The condition is autosomal recessive; heterozygous carriers may have milder temporary odour with high dietary choline intake.

Ambient heat and humidity increase both eccrine and apocrine secretion rates and accelerate bacterial growth, amplifying odour production. People living or working in hot climates require more frequent bathing and more aggressive antiperspirant or antibacterial strategies.

Hyperglycaemia and ketosis produce ketonic volatiles excreted through eccrine sweat. Optimising glycaemic control resolves this component; the condition acts as a clinical marker of inadequate glucose management.

affectedDemographics: Adolescents and young adults aged 15–35 when apocrine gland activity is highest
Individuals with primary hyperhidrosis, which amplifies bacterial odour production
People of African or European ancestry with the wet-earwax ABCC11 genotype
Overweight or obese individuals with prominent skin folds
Patients with trimethylaminuria, hepatic failure, renal failure, or poorly controlled diabetes
Athletes and individuals with occupational heat exposure who sweat heavily throughout the day
diagnosisOverview: Bromhidrosis is primarily a clinical diagnosis confirmed by patient history, physical examination, and exclusion of systemic or metabolic causes. The clinician characterises the odour type — axillary, fishy, sweet-ketonic, or ammonia-like — and its distribution (localised to apocrine areas versus generalised), and enquires about onset, exacerbating factors, menstrual cycle association, diet, medications, and family history. A skin swab from the affected area identifies the predominant bacterial species, which guides antibiotic or antiseptic selection. Urine trimethylamine-to-trimethylamine N-oxide ratio (typically measured after a choline challenge meal) is the definitive test for fish-odour syndrome. Fasting glucose and HbA1c are checked when a sweet or ketonic odour is present. Liver function tests, urea, and creatinine are measured when hepatic or renal causes are suspected. A brief psychological screen (GAD-7 for anxiety, PHQ-9 for depression) is important because the psychosocial burden is often severe and underrecognised. Olfactory reference syndrome — a psychiatric condition in which patients believe they have an odour that no one else can detect — must be distinguished from genuine bromhidrosis. When the clinician cannot detect any odour, assessment by a psychiatrist should be offered sensitively. Dermoscopy of the axilla may reveal Corynebacterium fluorescence under Wood's light (coral pink), and culture identifies antibiotic-resistant organisms.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical odour assessment and Wood's lamp examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the presence and character of bromhidrosis; Wood's lamp (UV light) reveals coral-pink fluorescence in the axilla indicating Corynebacterium species, the primary bacterial contributor to apocrine bromhidrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The dermatologist examines the affected areas without preparation; Wood's lamp is a handheld UV torch used in a darkened room. Takes 5 minutes and is entirely painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Axillary skin swab and bacterial culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the predominant bacteria and antibiotic sensitivities to guide targeted topical or systemic antibiotic or antiseptic treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cotton swab is rolled over the axillary skin; mild discomfort or tickling only. Culture results return in 2–3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive test for fish-odour syndrome; a high TMA-to-TMAO ratio after a standard choline load (300 mg oral choline or a marine food challenge) confirms FMO3 deficiency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You eat a choline-rich meal (eggs, fish, or a measured supplement) and collect urine over the following 24 hours. A blood sample may also be taken. Results in 5–10 working days at a metabolic laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out poorly controlled diabetes as a cause of sweet or ketonic odour, and identifies a reversible metabolic contributor.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Overnight fasting blood draw; results available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and renal function panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hepatic or renal failure as causes of generalised eccrine bromhidrosis with ammonia-like (uraemic) or sulphurous odour.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; no fasting required for liver function. Results in 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Sweat chloride and amino acid screen (if metabolic cause suspected)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies rare metabolic disorders including phenylketonuria, tyrosinaemia, and cystinuria that produce eccrine bromhidrosis in childhood; sweat chloride testing also excludes cystic fibrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sweat is collected from the forearm using a pilocarpine iontophoresis pad for 30 minutes, then analysed in a laboratory. A blood amino acid chromatography panel is taken from the same visit.

treatmentOverview: Treatment targets bacterial load reduction, apocrine secretion control, and any underlying systemic cause. First-line management for apocrine bromhidrosis is optimised hygiene: daily or twice-daily cleansing of apocrine areas with antibacterial soap (chlorhexidine 4%, benzoyl peroxide wash, or triclocarban-containing soap), combined with regular axillary hair shaving to reduce bacterial surface area. Topical aluminium-based antiperspirants (aluminium chloride hexahydrate 12–25%) applied to dry skin at night plug eccrine and apocrine duct openings, markedly reducing the substrate available for bacterial metabolism; they are the most evidence-supported first-line pharmacological intervention. When antiperspirants alone are insufficient, topical antibiotics — clindamycin 1% solution or erythromycin 2% gel — reduce Corynebacterium and staphylococcal populations in the axilla, typically producing improvement within 2–4 weeks. Topical antibacterials such as benzoyl peroxide 5–10% gel and metronidazole gel are alternatives that reduce resistance risk. Botulinum toxin A injections (50–100 units per axilla) suppress both eccrine and apocrine secretion by blocking cholinergic neurotransmission to sweat glands; improvement lasts 4–9 months and can be repeated. For severe or refractory apocrine bromhidrosis that does not respond to conservative measures, surgical apocrine gland removal — by superficial liposuction-curettage, subcutaneous shave excision, or laser subcision — offers the most durable solution. For trimethylaminuria, a low-choline diet and a brief rotating course of gut-decontaminating antibiotics (metronidazole, neomycin) to reduce intestinal TMA production can significantly reduce odour. Metabolic and organ failure causes require management of the underlying disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Aluminium chloride hexahydrate antiperspirant (12–25%)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to completely dry skin at bedtime and washed off in the morning; aluminium salts polymerise within eccrine and apocrine duct lumens, forming a physical plug that reduces secretion. Higher concentrations (Driclor, Certain Dri) are more effective than over-the-counter formulations. Mild irritation and stinging are common initially and resolve with continued use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces axillary odour in approximately 70–90% of patients with apocrine bromhidrosis when used consistently at prescription concentrations; the most robustly supported first-line topical agent.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical clindamycin 1% solution or erythromycin 2% gel
medicalTreatments.description: Antibiotics applied once or twice daily to affected apocrine areas reduce Corynebacterium and staphylococcal populations, directly decreasing bacterial odour production. Resistance may develop with prolonged use; benzoyl peroxide should be alternated to limit this.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces axillary odour scores by approximately 40–60% in controlled trials; most effective when combined with antiperspirants.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin A injections (axillary)
medicalTreatments.description: Intradermal injections of 50–100 units (onabotulinum toxin A) per axilla block cholinergic neurotransmission to both eccrine and apocrine glands, suppressing secretion and thereby reducing the substrate for bacterial odour production. Repeated every 4–9 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hyperhidrosis severity score by approximately 85–90% at 4 weeks and reduces bromhidrosis odour severity in parallel; FDA-approved for severe primary axillary hyperhidrosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antibiotics (short courses)
medicalTreatments.description: Erythromycin or tetracycline given orally for 4–6 weeks can substantially reduce axillary and groin Corynebacterium populations; reserved for severe initial presentation or when topical antibiotics fail. Not recommended as long-term maintenance due to resistance risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces clinically meaningful reduction in odour in approximately 60–75% of patients in the short term; sustained benefit requires maintenance hygiene.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-choline diet and gut-sterilising antibiotics (trimethylaminuria)
medicalTreatments.description: Reducing dietary choline, lecithin, and carnitine (found in eggs, liver, legumes, red meat, and fish) limits the substrate for intestinal TMA production; short rotating courses of metronidazole or neomycin reduce intestinal bacterial TMA synthesis. Riboflavin supplementation (B2, 30 mg three times daily) may enhance residual FMO3 activity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces urine and sweat TMA concentration and fish odour intensity in approximately 50–70% of trimethylaminuria patients on a combined dietary and antibiotic regimen.

surgicalOptions.name: Superficial liposuction-curettage of axillary apocrine glands
surgicalOptions.description: A small incision allows a curette or liposuction cannula to mechanically remove the subdermal apocrine gland layer under local or tumescent anaesthesia; most of the axillary apocrine glands are destroyed or removed in a single procedure. Minimal scarring; 2–3 weeks' recovery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Permanent odour elimination in approximately 80–90% of cases in large Japanese case series; the most widely performed definitive surgical approach in Asia.

surgicalOptions.name: Subcutaneous shave excision
surgicalOptions.description: A direct surgical excision of the subcutaneous tissue layer containing apocrine glands through a small axillary incision, performed under local anaesthesia. More invasive than liposuction-curettage; small but real risk of wound complications in 5–10% of cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term odour control in approximately 75–85% of patients; recurrence can occur if residual gland tissue remains.

surgicalOptions.name: MiraDry (microwave-based gland ablation)
surgicalOptions.description: A non-incisional device that delivers focused microwave energy to the interface of the dermis and subcutaneous fat, irreversibly ablating eccrine and apocrine glands. FDA-cleared for axillary hyperhidrosis and odour reduction; 1–2 treatment sessions required.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Studies report approximately 82–90% reduction in sweating and significant odour improvement at 12 months; permanent gland destruction in the treated area.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotics (oral and topical)
alternativeTreatments.description: Oral Lactobacillus species and topical bacteriotherapy aim to competitively displace odour-producing Corynebacterium and staphylococci with non-odour-producing skin commensals. Small studies show promise, but evidence is preliminary and no standardised product is approved.

alternativeTreatments.name: Zinc supplement (oral, 25–50 mg daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Zinc has antibacterial properties and may reduce sweat odour through an unknown mechanism; small trials report odour reduction with oral zinc in some patients. Well tolerated at standard doses; evidence base is limited.

alternativeTreatments.name: Chlorophyllin (oral)
alternativeTreatments.description: An oral internal deodorant derived from chlorophyll that binds trimethylamine and other volatile amine compounds in the gut, reducing their systemic absorption; used at 100–300 mg daily in trimethylaminuria. Limited formal trial data but used in clinical practice for decades.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be consulted when over-the-counter antiperspirants and daily hygiene fail to control odour within 4–6 weeks, when the odour is generalised rather than limited to axillary areas, or when a systemic cause is suspected. Metabolic causes (trimethylaminuria, renal, or hepatic failure) require specialist investigation and treatment that dermatologists may need to co-ordinate with metabolic physicians, nephrologists, or hepatologists. Botulinum toxin injections and surgical procedures require specialist delivery and follow-up.
doctorSelectionGuide: A dermatologist with experience in hyperhidrosis and sweat disorders is the most appropriate first specialist. For surgical options, seek a dermatological surgeon or plastic surgeon with specific experience in axillary apocrine gland removal or MiraDry procedures. For suspected trimethylaminuria, a metabolic physician or specialist in inherited metabolic disease can perform definitive enzymatic and genetic testing. If significant anxiety, depression, or features of olfactory reference syndrome are present, a psychiatrist or psychologist experienced in dermatological conditions (psychodermatology) is an important addition to the care team.
preventionStrategies: Bathe or shower at least once daily, with targeted cleansing of the axillae, groin, and perianal area using an antibacterial wash (chlorhexidine or benzoyl peroxide).
Use prescription-strength aluminium chloride antiperspirant nightly on the axillae if over-the-counter products are insufficient — this is the most effective single hygiene measure.
Shave axillary hair regularly to reduce bacterial colonisable surface area by threefold to fivefold.
Wear natural-fibre (cotton, bamboo) or moisture-wicking athletic fabrics that allow ventilation rather than trapping moisture and heat against apocrine areas.
Wash clothing thoroughly after each wear; residual fatty acid metabolites in clothing fibres re-deposit on skin and re-inoculate bacteria.
For trimethylaminuria, maintain a sustained low-choline diet and consult a dietitian trained in inherited metabolic disease.
lifestyleModifications: Shower immediately after exercise, heat exposure, or any period of heavy sweating — bacterial odour production increases exponentially within 2–4 hours of apocrine secretion.
Carry antibacterial wipes (chlorhexidine-based) for use on the axillae and groin at midday on warm or physically active days, as a pragmatic bridge between showers.
Reduce intake of dietary contributors to systemic eccrine bromhidrosis: garlic, onion, fenugreek, alcohol, and very high-protein or choline-rich diets.
Change and wash clothing, particularly shirts and underwear, after every use; fabric softener alone is insufficient to neutralise volatile fatty acid residues.

Keep a symptom diary noting odour severity on a 1–10 scale by day, diet, temperature, and stress level — this identifies specific dietary or behavioural triggers and documents treatment response objectively.
Address associated hyperhidrosis if present — treating excessive sweating with aluminium chloride or botulinum toxin substantially reduces bromhidrosis in co-affected patients.

recommended: Fresh fruits and vegetables high in antioxidants, which do not contribute volatile odorants to sweat
Adequate hydration to dilute eccrine secretions and reduce volatile compound concentration in sweat
Probiotic foods (yoghurt, kefir, fermented vegetables) to support a healthy gut microbiome that metabolises dietary amine precursors more efficiently
Low-choline diet (avoiding egg yolks, liver, fish, legumes, and lecithin supplements) in patients with confirmed or suspected trimethylaminuria
avoid: Garlic, onion, leeks, and shallots (contain allicin and diallyl disulphide that are excreted through eccrine sweat)
Fenugreek and asafoetida spices, which produce sotolon excretion through sweat (caramel-like or maple odour)
Large amounts of alcohol, which is metabolised to acetaldehyde and other volatile compounds excreted through skin and breath
High-choline foods (egg yolks, liver, cod, shellfish, lentils, and lecithin) in trimethylaminuria patients

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is strongly encouraged for overall health and does not cause bromhidrosis to worsen permanently, but requires prompt post-exercise hygiene. Shower within 30 minutes of stopping exercise and reapply antiperspirant. Choose breathable moisture-wicking fabrics and change out of damp clothing immediately after workouts. In patients with confirmed hyperhidrosis contributing to bromhidrosis, aquatic exercise provides a natural cooling and cleansing environment. Botulinum toxin treatments allow athletes to exercise without significant odour-related impairment for 4–9 months between injections.
dailyManagement: Apply prescription aluminium chloride antiperspirant to completely dry axillary skin at bedtime, leave for 6–8 hours (overnight), and wash off in the morning — this routine is most effective when started before sweating season.
Shower at least once daily with a chlorhexidine or benzoyl peroxide wash; rinse all soap residues thoroughly, since residual alkaline pH promotes bacterial growth.
Shave axillary hair at least weekly, or use a depilatory cream, to minimise bacterial colonisation surface area.
Change clothing at least once daily and more frequently during hot weather or after exercise; do not re-wear unwashed shirts regardless of visual cleanliness.
Monitor odour response to treatments using a consistent self-rating scale and note changes after dietary modifications, heat exposure, or medication changes to identify patterns.

Contact the prescribing clinician promptly if topical treatment causes skin irritation, rash, or ulceration — hypersensitivity to aluminium chloride or topical antibiotics is occasionally seen and can be managed by switching formulations.
prognosis: The prognosis of apocrine bromhidrosis is excellent with appropriate management. The majority of patients achieve satisfactory odour control with the combination of optimised hygiene, prescription aluminium chloride antiperspirants, and topical antibiotics. Botulinum toxin injections provide 4–9 months of marked improvement per treatment cycle and can be repeated indefinitely. Surgical apocrine gland removal is curative in 80–90% of carefully selected patients and is rarely followed by significant recurrence. Eccrine bromhidrosis from dietary causes resolves completely once the dietary trigger is avoided. Trimethylaminuria-associated odour is controllable but not curable with current therapies; most patients can achieve a social quality of life acceptable to them with sustained dietary restriction and intermittent antibiotic courses. The psychological consequences of bromhidrosis — anxiety, depression, and social withdrawal — may persist even after odour is controlled and merit independent psychological support. Odour-related stigma is significant and recovery of social confidence often lags physical treatment response.
recoveryTimeline: Topical aluminium chloride produces noticeable odour reduction within 1–2 weeks of nightly application, with full effect at 4–6 weeks. Topical antibiotics show improvement within 2–4 weeks. Botulinum toxin axillary injections reduce odour within 2–5 days of treatment and maintain effect for 4–9 months. Surgical liposuction-curettage or shave excision procedures require 2–3 weeks of wound healing, with final cosmetic and functional results at 4–8 weeks; the odour benefit is typically permanent. Dietary restriction in trimethylaminuria reduces fishy odour within 48–72 hours of eliminating high-choline foods.
complications: Skin irritation, contact dermatitis, or superficial ulceration at antiperspirant application sites, particularly with high-concentration aluminium chloride; managed by reducing frequency or switching to a lower concentration.
Secondary bacterial skin infection (intertrigo, erythrasma, folliculitis) in chronically moist apocrine fold areas, requiring targeted topical or systemic antibiotic treatment.

Psychological complications — anxiety disorder, social phobia, depression, and occupational impairment are documented in the majority of patients with severe bromhidrosis in survey studies; these require dedicated psychological or psychiatric management.

Olfactory reference syndrome — a small proportion of patients who seek treatment do not have bromhidrosis detectable by others but have a delusional or obsessive belief that they do; this psychiatric condition requires psychological or psychiatric intervention rather than dermatological procedures.

Surgical complications — wound infection, haematoma, sensory changes, and minor scarring are reported in approximately 5–10% of axillary apocrine gland surgeries; choose experienced surgeons and follow post-operative hygiene carefully.

supportResources.name: International Hyperhidrosis Society (IHHS)
supportResources.description: Patient and clinician resources on hyperhidrosis and bromhidrosis, including treatment guides, doctor finder tools, and support community.

supportResources.name: Trimethylaminuria (TMAU) Support Network
supportResources.description: UK-based patient community and support group for individuals with fish-odour syndrome (trimethylaminuria), providing dietary guides and peer support.

supportResources.name: DermNet NZ — Bromhidrosis
supportResources.description: Physician-authored, peer-reviewed dermatology reference on bromhidrosis with patient-friendly summaries, images, and management guidance.

sources.title: Fredricks DN. Microbial ecology of human skin in health and disease. Journal of Investigative Dermatology Symposium Proceedings, 2001
Shelley WB, Hurley HJ. The physiology of the human axillary apocrine sweat gland. Journal of Investigative Dermatology, 1953
Barzilai DA et al. Comparison of topical and oral erythromycin for treatment of bromhidrosis. Cutis, 2004
Whittle CL et al. Human axillary secretions influence women's perception of men's attractiveness. Chemical Senses, 1998 — and references therein on axillary odour compounds
Treacy EP et al. Mutations of the flavin-containing monooxygenase gene (FMO3) cause trimethylaminuria, a defect in detoxication. Human Molecular Genetics, 1998
Felber J et al. Botulinum toxin for axillary bromhidrosis and hyperhidrosis: a comparative review. Journal of Cosmetic Dermatology, 2020
Nakashima H et al. Subcutaneous liposuction-curettage for axillary bromhidrosis: a long-term outcome study. Aesthetic Plastic Surgery, 2019
Yokota N et al. ABCC11 genotype is associated with axillary osmidrosis susceptibility. Molecular Genetics and Genomics, 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Hidradenitis suppurativa
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hidradenitis suppurativa causes painful nodules, abscesses, and sinus tracts in apocrine-dense areas and may produce offensive discharge, but the defining feature is the painful inflammatory lesions, not bacterial decomposition of apocrine secretions; the two conditions can co-occur but have distinct pathophysiology and management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Erythrasma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Erythrasma is a Corynebacterium minutissimum infection producing a brown, scaly rash in skin folds with mild odour; it is detected by coral-pink Wood's lamp fluorescence and treated with topical or oral erythromycin, distinguishing it from primary bromhidrosis in which the skin surface is structurally normal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trimethylaminuria (fish-odour syndrome)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trimethylaminuria is a metabolic disorder producing systemic eccrine bromhidrosis from FMO3 enzyme deficiency, with a distinctive fishy odour affecting breath, urine, and all sweat glands; it requires dietary and metabolic management rather than topical dermatological treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinea pedis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinea pedis (athlete's foot) is a fungal infection of the skin between the toes and sole that produces a characteristic malodour; it is distinguished by scaling, maceration, and intense pruritus and responds to topical antifungals, whereas plantar bromhidrosis involves bacterial rather than fungal odour production.

relatedConditions.name: Hidradenitis suppurativa
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares apocrine gland involvement and produces offensive discharge; the two conditions can coexist in the same patient and both benefit from Corynebacterium-targeted antibiotic therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Trimethylaminuria
relatedConditions.relevance: The most important systemic cause of eccrine bromhidrosis, producing a distinctive fishy body odour from impaired hepatic metabolism of dietary trimethylamine.

relatedConditions.name: Erythrasma
relatedConditions.relevance: A Corynebacterium infection in skin folds that shares the same bacterial genus as the primary odour-producing organisms in apocrine bromhidrosis; erythrasma and bromhidrosis can coexist in the same axilla.

relatedConditions.name: Tinea pedis
relatedConditions.relevance: Foot fungal infection produces its own odour chemistry and may co-occur with plantar bromhidrosis; distinguishing the two directs treatment toward antifungals versus antibacterial antiperspirants.

faqs.question: What is bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Bromhidrosis is a condition in which the sweat produced by the body has an unusually strong, offensive odour caused by bacterial decomposition of sweat secretions on the skin surface. It most commonly affects the armpits, groin, and feet, where special apocrine sweat glands produce secretions that bacteria convert into malodorous compounds.

faqs.question: What causes bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Apocrine bromhidrosis — the most common form — is caused by skin bacteria (mainly Corynebacterium and staphylococci) breaking down oily apocrine sweat secretions in the armpits and groin into volatile fatty acids and thioalcohols. Eccrine bromhidrosis results from metabolic disorders such as trimethylaminuria, poorly controlled diabetes, or ingestion of odour-producing foods including garlic and alcohol.

faqs.question: Is bromhidrosis the same as body odour?
faqs.answer: Normal body odour is part of everyday physiology; bromhidrosis is a clinically significant, persistent, and often severely distressing odour that returns within hours of bathing, cannot be controlled by standard deodorants, and affects quality of life. The underlying mechanisms are the same, but the degree and persistency are abnormal.

faqs.question: How is bromhidrosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A dermatologist confirms bromhidrosis by clinical examination, Wood's lamp inspection of the axilla for Corynebacterium fluorescence, and bacterial swab culture. When systemic causes are suspected, urine trimethylamine testing screens for fish-odour syndrome; blood glucose checks for diabetes; and liver and kidney function tests screen for organ failure.

faqs.question: Can bromhidrosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Apocrine bromhidrosis can be effectively controlled with consistent hygiene, prescription antiperspirants, and topical antibiotics in most patients. Surgical removal of axillary apocrine glands eliminates the odour permanently in 80–90% of cases. Eccrine forms from dietary causes resolve once the food is eliminated; trimethylaminuria is manageable but not currently curable.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: The most effective combination is daily antibacterial washing (chlorhexidine or benzoyl peroxide), nightly prescription aluminium chloride antiperspirant, and axillary hair removal. Topical clindamycin reduces Corynebacterium levels. Botulinum toxin injections suppress apocrine secretion for 4–9 months. Surgical apocrine gland removal is the most permanent solution.

faqs.question: Does shaving armpits help bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Axillary hair increases the surface area available for bacterial colonisation by three to five times. Shaving or using a depilatory cream regularly reduces the bacterial population, improves antiperspirant contact with skin, and measurably reduces axillary odour. It is one of the simplest and most evidence-supported measures.

faqs.question: Does botulinum toxin help bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Botulinum toxin A (Botox) injected into the armpits blocks the nerve signal to both eccrine and apocrine sweat glands, markedly reducing secretion and the substrate for bacterial odour. Improvement lasts 4–9 months and can be repeated. It is FDA-approved for axillary hyperhidrosis and widely used off-label for bromhidrosis.

faqs.question: What is fish-odour syndrome?
faqs.answer: Fish-odour syndrome (trimethylaminuria) is a metabolic disorder in which the liver cannot convert trimethylamine — produced by gut bacteria from dietary choline — into its odourless form. Unprocessed trimethylamine is excreted through sweat, urine, and breath, creating a persistent fishy smell. It is managed with a low-choline diet, gut antibiotics, and oral chlorophyllin.

faqs.question: Can diet cause bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Garlic, onion, fenugreek, alcohol, and very high-choline foods (eggs, liver, fish) all contribute volatile compounds that are absorbed, transported in the bloodstream, and excreted through eccrine sweat, producing a characteristic odour. This dietary eccrine bromhidrosis resolves within 24–48 hours of avoiding the trigger foods.

faqs.question: Is bromhidrosis surgery safe?
faqs.answer: Axillary liposuction-curettage and shave excision are well-established procedures with good safety profiles. Reported complication rates are 5–10% and include minor wound infection, haematoma, and small areas of altered sensation. These procedures are typically performed under local anaesthesia. Choosing a surgeon experienced in apocrine gland surgery significantly reduces complication risk.

faqs.question: Can bromhidrosis affect mental health?
faqs.answer: Yes, significantly. Survey studies show that severe bromhidrosis is associated with anxiety, social phobia, depression, and occupational impairment in the majority of affected patients. The psychological burden often exceeds that of many visible skin diseases. Psychological support — including cognitive behavioural therapy — should be offered alongside dermatological treatment.

faqs.question: Does bromhidrosis worsen with hyperhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Excessive sweating (hyperhidrosis) dramatically amplifies bromhidrosis by creating a persistently moist, warm skin surface that accelerates bacterial growth and metabolite production. Treating hyperhidrosis with prescription aluminium chloride, botulinum toxin, or other measures typically produces a proportionate reduction in associated odour.

faqs.question: Is bromhidrosis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Bromhidrosis is not contagious or infectious. It results from the interaction of normal skin bacteria with an individual's own sweat secretions. The bacteria involved are common skin commensals; the odour is produced on the affected person's skin and cannot be transmitted to others by contact.

faqs.question: How do aluminium antiperspirants work for bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: When applied to dry skin, aluminium salts dissolve in eccrine sweat and polymerise into a gel that physically plugs sweat duct openings, reducing secretion from both eccrine and apocrine glands. Less sweat means less substrate for bacteria to convert into malodorous compounds. Prescription concentrations (15–25%) are significantly more effective than over-the-counter products (2–5%).

faqs.question: What is the difference between apocrine and eccrine bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Apocrine bromhidrosis originates in the large apocrine glands of the armpits and groin, where bacteria decompose oily secretions into volatile fatty acids. Eccrine bromhidrosis occurs when volatile malodorous compounds are excreted through the numerous body-surface eccrine glands — most often from metabolic disorders (trimethylaminuria), organ failure, or ingested foods. Apocrine forms are far more common.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for body odour?
faqs.answer: See a dermatologist when body odour is persistent despite daily antibacterial bathing and prescription antiperspirants, when the odour is generalised rather than confined to the armpits, when it appears to have a fishy or sweet-ketonic character, or when it is causing significant social or occupational impairment. A clinician can exclude systemic causes and offer treatments unavailable over the counter.

faqs.question: Does MiraDry work for bromhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. MiraDry uses microwave energy to permanently destroy eccrine and apocrine glands in the axilla. Clinical studies report approximately 82–90% reduction in sweating and significant odour improvement at 12 months, with the effects being permanent in the treated area. It requires 1–2 treatment sessions. Some swelling and discomfort occur for 1–2 weeks after treatment.

faqs.question: Can bromhidrosis affect the feet?
faqs.answer: Yes. Plantar bromhidrosis results from bacterial decomposition of eccrine secretions on the sole and between the toes, producing isovaleric acid and short-chain fatty acids with a characteristic cheesy odour. Treatment includes antibacterial foot soaks, prescription aluminium chloride foot antiperspirant, moisture-wicking socks, and daily footwear ventilation.

faqs.question: Is bromhidrosis more common in certain ethnicities?
faqs.answer: Yes. Apocrine bromhidrosis is far less common in people of East Asian ancestry because approximately 98% carry the dry-earwax variant of the ABCC11 gene, which confers much lower apocrine secretion activity. Populations of African and European ancestry are predominantly wet-earwax carriers and have significantly higher apocrine activity and bromhidrosis prevalence.

metaDescription: Bromhidrosis causes persistent offensive body odour from bacterial sweat decomposition. Learn about antiperspirant, botulinum toxin, and surgical treatments.
